elements.sent-2411-001: We derive the quantitative propagation of chaos in the sense of relative entropy for the 2D viscous vortex model with general circulations, approximating the vorticity formulation of the 2D Navier-Stokes equation on the whole Euclidean space, which is an extension of the previous works \cite{fournier2014propagation,jabin2018quantitative .}
elements.sent-2411-002: We improve our previous results in \cite{feng2023quantitative to the more general case that the vorticity may change sign, allowing the circulations to be in different orientations.}

elements.sent-2411-004: Both results of convergence rates are global-in-time on any finite time horizon and optimal among existing literature, thanks to the careful estimates using the Grigor'yan parabolic maximum principle and a new ODE hierarchy and iterated integral estimates.
elements.sent-2411-005: Consider the $N$-particle point vortex system positioning on the whole Euclidean space $\R^2$ with general circulations for vortices, whose evolution is governed by the following stochastic differential equations (SDEs): \begin{equation}\label{particle} \ud X_i(t)=\frac{1}{N} \sum_{j \neq i} \M_j \cdot K(X_i-X_j)\ud t +\sqrt{2\sigma} \ud B_i(t), \quad 1 \leq i \leq N.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-006: The particles are labeled by the index $i$ ranging from $1$ to $N$, with $X_i(t) \in \R^2$ and $\M_i \in \R$ representing respectively the position and the circulation of the $i$-th vortex.
elements.sent-2411-007: Here $\lbrace B_i(t) \rbrace_{1 \leq i \leq N}$ are $N$ independent copies of the standard 2D Brownian motion.
elements.sent-2411-008: The constant $\sigma>0$ in the diffusion term represents the viscosity of the dynamics of the system.
elements.sent-2411-009: As we can see from our SDE system \eqref{particle , the evolution of the position of each particle is driven by a deterministic drift term, in the form of pairwise interacting force with some weights given by the circulations of other particles, and an extra stochastic diffusion term given by the additive noise with constant strength.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The different cases of circulations with $\M_i>0$ and $\M_i<0$ represent the two different orientations of the point vortices.
elements.sent-2411-011: We mention that the scale $1/N$ appearing in the vortex circulation is in the sense of mean-field scaling, keeping the whole circulation of the system bounded.
elements.sent-2411-012: The singular interacting kernel $K$ has many different options arising from various natural physical models.

elements.subsection-2411-001: The Liouville Equation, BBGKY Hierarchy and the Limit Equation
elements.sent-2411-035: In this subsection, we present the corresponding Liouville (master) equation (forward Kolmogorov equation) and the BBGKY hierarchy of the SDE system \eqref{particle and the nonlinear Fokker-Planck equation of the limit system \eqref{limit , since our method will always work on the PDE level.}}

elements.sent-2411-079: Propagation of chaos and mean-field limit for large systems of interacting particles, widely appearing in analysis of PDEs and probability theory, has been extensively studied over the last decades, both for the first-order and second-order systems.
elements.sent-2411-080: The derivation of some effective equations to describe the average or statistical behavior of large interacting particle systems can be traced back to Maxwell and Boltzmann.
elements.sent-2411-081: Kac \cite{kac1956foundations first raises the basic notions of chaos and proves some partial propagation of chaos results for the simplified model of 1D Boltzmann equation, and now the related studies have been far beyond the range of kinetic theory.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The fundamental mathematical setting of this problem is set up and basically resolved by McKean \cite{mckean1967propagation and Dobrushin \cite{dobrushin1979vlasov using the classical coupling method.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Since then people began to put attention to the much more difficult models, say systems with singular interacting kernels, instead of the regular (bounded Lipschitz) kernels.
elements.sent-2411-084: As for our point vortex model approximating the famous 2D Navier-Stokes/Euler equation, the first result dates back to Osada \cite{osada1986propagation with bounded initial data and large viscosity.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Later Fournier-Hauray-Mischler \cite{fournier2014propagation extend the result to all positive viscosity cases, while the initial data is only assumed to have finite moments and entropy, using the compactness argument and the entropy estimate.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The relative entropy method as in Jabin-Wang \cite{jabin2016mean,jabin2018quantitative has seen great effectiveness in solving quantitative propagation of chaos results for McKean-Vlasov process in recent years, especially making sense for the singular interacting kernel cases.}

elements.sent-2411-088: In \cite{jabin2018quantitative they successfully applied to the 2D Navier-Stokes equation with the singular kernel given by the Biot-Savart law, but restricted to the torus case and without general circulations.}

elements.sent-2411-388: We derive quantitative propagation of chaos in the sense of relative entropy for the 2D viscous vortex model with general circulations, approximating the vorticity formulation of the 2D Navier-Stokes equation on the whole Euclidean space, which is an extension of the previous works \cite{fournier2014propagation,jabin2018quantitative .}
elements.sent-2411-389: Both results of convergence rates are global-in-time on any finite time horizon and optimal among existing literature, thanks to the sharp logarithmic growth estimates and a new ODE hierarchy and iterated integral estimates.
elements.sent-2411-390: Consider the $N$-particle stochastic point vortex system positioning on the whole Euclidean space $\R^2$ with general circulations for vortices, whose evolution is governed by the following stochastic differential equations (SDEs): \begin{equation}\label{particle} \ud X_i(t)=\frac{1}{N} \sum_{j \neq i} \M_j \cdot K(X_i-X_j)\ud t +\sqrt{2\sigma} \ud B_i(t), \quad 1 \leq i \leq N.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-391: The particles are labeled with the index $i$ ranging from $1$ to $N$, with $X_i(t) \in \R^2$ and $\M_i/N \in \R$ representing, respectively, the position and circulation of the $i$-th vortex.
elements.sent-2411-392: The displacement of the particles is determined by the combination effect of two factors.
elements.sent-2411-393: The first one is given by the pairwise interaction force with some weights given by the circulations of other particles.
elements.sent-2411-394: We mention that the scale $1/N$ that appears in the vortex circulation is in the sense of mean-field scaling, keeping the whole circulation of the system bounded.
elements.sent-2411-395: The second one is given by the extra additive noise, driven by $N$ independent copies of the standard 2D Brownian motion, which are denoted by $\lbrace B_i(t) \rbrace_{1 \leq i \leq N}$.
elements.sent-2411-396: The constant $\sigma>0$ represents the viscosity of the system dynamics and measures the intensity of the diffusion.
elements.sent-2411-397: The interacting kernel $K$ has many different options arising from various natural physical models.
elements.sent-2411-398: This point vortex model was initiated in the study of Helmholtz \cite{helmholtz1858uber and Kirchhoff \cite{kirchhoff1876vorlesungen .}}
elements.sent-2411-399: It was Osada \cite{osada1986propagation who first showed that, although the kernel has some singularity of $O(1/|x|)$ at the origin, which makes the interaction between two particles large enough when they become sufficiently close to each other, the particles actually never collide in the evolution process with probability one.}
elements.sent-2411-400: Therefore, the singularity of the system will never be reached, and the well-posedness of \eqref{particle is justified.}
elements.sent-2411-401: This allows us to use the convention that $K(0)=0$, and thus add the terms with $i=j$ to the summation in \eqref{particle .}
elements.sent-2411-402: Throughout this article, we make the common assumption that $\M_i$ is independent of time $t$, which means that the circulation of each point vortex, once determined at the initial time $t=0$, remains unchanged as the system evolves in time.
elements.sent-2411-403: This new point vortex system \eqref{scaleparticle is endowed with scaled circulations $\Tilde{\M _j=\M_j/A$, which are supported in $[-1,1]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-404: Therefore, we can always change our target system to satisfy the additional assumption $A \leq 1$, at the price that the viscosity constant $\sigma$ is also rescaled by the constant $A$, which is consistent with our assumptions on $\sigma$ in the main results.
elements.sent-2411-405: This setting is a generalized version of the previous work of the authors \cite{feng2023quantitative , where the point vortex system has fixed circulations $\M_i \equiv 1$ at each vortex $X_i$ and thus the particle system is symmetric in position $X_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-406: We are interested in the mean-field limit dynamics of the point vortex system \eqref{particle when $N \rightarrow \infty$ in some appropriate sense of statistical mechanics.}
elements.sent-2411-407: It is well-known that the limit self-consistent McKean-Vlasov system writes \begin{equation}\label{limit} \ud X_t=(K \ast \omega_t)(X_t)+\sqrt{2\sigma}\ud B_t,\end{equation} where $X_t \in \R^2$ and $B_t$ is a standard 2D Brownian motion, and the vorticity function \begin{equation}\omega_t(x)=\omega(t,x)=\E_\M g_t(\M, X_t)=\int_\R mg_t(m,x) \ud m\end{equation} is the expectation of $g_t(m,x)$ with respect to $m$, where \begin{equation}g_t(m,x)=\text{Law}(\M,X_t)\end{equation} represents the joint density of $\M$ and $X_t$.
